title: The Faithful Parent: Intro
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-10-23 - The Faithful Parent - Intro - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Study Parenting as a Church
subpoints: The church is commanded to hear all of God's instruction together
Discipleship grows cross-generationally

outline.title: The Goal of Parenting: The Glory of God
subpoints: The glory of God = the weight of His presence and character manifested
To glorify God is to display His character truthfully in all we do
Parenting for God's glory acknowledges and declares His presence

outline.title: A Definition of Faithful Parenting
subpoints: Faithful parenting = faithfulness to God's Word by His grace and for His glory
Only one perfectly faithful parent exists: God the Father
All parents need help; God's grace is sufficient
Only regenerate parents can be truly faithful parents

outline.title: The Biblical Responsibilities of Parents
subpoints: Training (paideia): structured guidance and restraint for external life
Admonition (nouthesia): placing truth in the mind and addressing the heart
Parents must not provoke children to wrath — 32 ways identified
Faithful parenting begins with the Word dwelling richly in parents themselves

outline.title: The Basic Biblical Responsibilities of Children
subpoints: Obedience: listening under (hupakouo) — listening with intent to follow
Honor: gracious and respectful attitude toward parents

outline.title: Our Hope in Parenting: The Gospel
subpoints: God's covenant promises extend to our children
The gospel must be present in every stage of parenting
Our greatest desire as parents is that our children walk faithfully with the Lord

outline.title: Overview of the Series and Recommended Resources
subpoints: Upcoming topics: parenting by age, special cases, when things don't go as planned
Fourteen recommended resources listed, including The Faithful Parent by Scott and Peace

summary: This introductory lesson launches the "Faithful Parent" Sunday school series on biblical parenting. Pastor Lyro begins by establishing that the whole church — not just parents — benefits from hearing God's instruction on parenting, since discipleship is a cross-generational endeavor. The goal of parenting is defined as the glory of God: doing all things in such a way that God's character and presence are displayed truthfully. A faithful parent is one who strives to be steadfast and reliable in their commitment to God's Word and to their children's good, by His grace. Pastor Lyro emphasizes that only parents born again by the Spirit of God can be truly faithful parents, since understanding and applying Scripture requires the Holy Spirit. The responsibilities of parents are grounded in Ephesians 6: training children externally (paideia) and admonishing them from the heart (nouthesia), while taking care not to provoke them to wrath through harshness, inconsistency, or self-centered parenting. Faithful parenting begins with the Word of God dwelling richly in the parents themselves. Children, in turn, bear the responsibilities of obedience and honor. The hope in all parenting is the gospel of Jesus Christ and God's covenant promises to be the God of His people and their children. The series concludes its introduction with an overview of upcoming topics and a list of fourteen recommended biblical resources on parenting.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Study Parenting as a Church

Today we are starting a series on parenting. A parenting series is a hard one to teach for a couple of reasons. One is that some of the things I've been suggesting, I wasn't able to practice with my own kids. And then you might think — okay, I understand for people with kids, but I passed the age of having kids, why should I be involved with this? Well, think about the book of Ephesians. The whole book — and the same with Colossians, the same with First Peter — they're given to the church, and the church was commanded to read it together. Everybody was supposed to listen to what each segment of the church was supposed to be doing. Paul doesn't say, when you get to the part on parents, excuse the rest of the people. No, everyone in the church was to hear God's instruction in every area of life. That makes a lot of sense because discipleship grows together. It happens cross-generationally as well. And we all need to be hearing what is pertinent to every person in the church.

So we're going to call the series "The Faithful Parent." I'm so uncreative that I couldn't even come up with this title by myself — I saw it as the name of a book by Stuart Scott and Martha Peace.

## The Goal of Parenting: The Glory of God

If you're in Ephesians 6, verses 1 through 4 — an amazingly familiar passage that sometimes we might even forget. "Children, obey your parents in the Lord for this is right. Honor your father and mother, which is the first commandment with promise, that it may be well with you, and you may live long on the earth. And you, fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath. Bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord."

Our goal for this morning is to talk about the biblical basis for parenting. And the goal of parenting is the glory of God. The glory of God literally in the Old Testament is the weight of His presence and His character manifested. The word in the Old Testament translated "glory" is really the word for weight. When the glory of God comes into the temple of Solomon in 1 Kings 8, the priests have to leave because of the weight of the glory of God coming into the sanctuary. So to do things for the glory of God is to do things in such a way that God's character and presence are displayed. When we give glory to God, we're not adding glory to Him — He already has infinite glory. But when we do things for the glory of God, we declare His presence and who He truthfully is.

So parenting that has the glory of God as its goal will declare what is true about God, and will be done in accordance with all that He says. We could also say that the goal of parenting is faithfulness to God's Word by His grace and for His glory.

## A Definition of Faithful Parenting

In all eternity, in the existence of everything, there's only been one perfectly faithful parent, and that's God the Father — and there has been only one perfectly faithful child, and that is the Son, the Lord Jesus Christ. Every other parent and child needs help.

Faithfulness in the Old Testament means: permanent, true, certain, of long-continuous steadfast, sure, trustworthy — carrying the idea of a firm and sure support. So faithful parents will be steadfast, trustworthy, and true concerning our commitment to God and His Word. Faithful parents will be like God in the sense that they are reliable in parental care and in commitment to their children's good. Faithfulness — not perfection — is what the Lord calls us to in parenting.

The Bible is sufficient for all the principles of parenting. The only parents that can be faithful parents are parents who have been born again by the Spirit of God, because only those parents are able to understand and access and use the Bible properly. The Apostle says in 1 Corinthians 2: "The natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God for they are foolishness to him, nor can he know them because they are spiritually discerned."

God's enabling grace is always available and sufficient. As Peter says in 2 Peter 1: "His divine power has given to us all things that pertain to life and godliness."

## The Biblical Responsibilities of Parents

Faithful parenting starts with the parents, not with the children. The word of God must dwell richly in the parents. Deuteronomy 6:6 says, "These words which I command you today shall be in your heart" — the parents are the ones who will then teach their children from that point. Unless the word of God is first in our hearts as parents, we're not going to be successful or faithful in our parenting.

The basic biblical responsibilities belong to the parents: "And you, fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4). There are two things there: training and admonition.

The word "training" translates the Greek word *paideia*, which has the basic idea of training one's external life — structured guidance and restraint, whether by practical guidelines, encouragement, reproof, or discipline appropriate to the age of the child. These are the railroad tracks that allow the train to go where it needs to go.

The word "admonition" translates *nouthesia*, which means to place or set in the mind — placing the mind straight. So in parenting, not only do we deal with the external, but we also deal with the heart. We want not just children who behave properly and do what's socially acceptable, but children who in the heart know who God is and who the Lord Jesus Christ is.

While training and admonishing our children, parents are not to provoke them to wrath. Colossians 3:21 says, "Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged." There are 32 ways a parent can habitually provoke their children to wrath — including demanding more than they can accomplish, being cruel, making fun of them, withholding love, lack of marital harmony, establishing a child-centered home, disciplining while angry, being inconsistent, having double standards, emphasizing man's laws rather than God's laws, not admitting you are wrong, and not asking for forgiveness.

## The Basic Biblical Responsibilities of Children

Children have responsibility to obey their parents in things agreeable to the Word of God. The original word for "obey" in Ephesians 6:1 is *hupakouo*, which literally means "to listen under" — listening with the intent of following through in obedience. And obedience leads logically to honor: a gracious and respectful attitude toward parents, both in front of them and when speaking about them.

## Our Hope in Parenting: The Gospel

Our hope in raising our children is God's covenant promises to be our God and the God of our children. In Acts 2, Peter says: "For the promise is to you and to your children and to all who are far off, as many as the Lord our God will call." Our hope is in the gospel of Jesus Christ. The gospel in its fullness must be present in all areas and stages of parenting. Your child is never too young to hear the gospel.

Brothers and sisters, our greatest desire as parents is that our children walk faithfully with the Lord all the days of their lives. If your primary desire in parenting is something else, you need to repent.

## Overview of the Series

Where are we going from here? We're going to talk about parenting in everyday life — the infant, the toddler, the preschooler, the school-aged child, the teenager. After that, we're going to shift and talk about some special cases: single parenting, divorced parents sharing custody, blended families, absent parenting, unequally yoked families, grandparenting issues, and parents who need children with special needs. And at the end, we'll talk about what to do when things don't go as planned — when you've done your best by God's grace to be faithful, and yet your child walks away from the Lord.

The outline for our series comes from the book *The Faithful Parent: A Biblical Guide to Raising a Family* by Martha Peace and Stuart Scott. Other recommended resources include *Shepherding a Child's Heart* by Tedd Tripp, *Parenting by God's Promises* by Joel Beeke, *Teach Them Diligently* by Lou Priolo, and *Age of Opportunity* by Paul Tripp for parenting teens.

Father in heaven, thank you that you are good. We thank you that you've given us your word, and in it we have everything that we need for faith and practice, including parenting. Help us to be faithful in raising your children in the church. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
